behaviors.description: The clause extractor identifies all key terms (parties, dates, obligations, penalties) present in the contract.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is whether the extractor correctly identifies terms — this is a single reviewer's output quality, not a multi-module handoff. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/subprocess dependency for extraction itself. LLM-DEP: The clause extractor is an AI reviewer. Whether it correctly identifies all key terms (including edge-case phrasings of parties, obligations, etc.) depends entirely on what a real LLM produces. A deterministic stub always 'passes' because you define its output — it cannot reveal that the model misses a penalty clause phrased unusually. MULTI-STEP: No; single reviewer step. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: Integration would only wire module boundaries; it cannot reveal whether the AI correctly identifies key terms in a real contract. A stub extractor always finds what you tell it to find.
behaviors.test_description: Feed a real contract containing parties, effective dates, payment obligations, and penalty clauses (some phrased unusually) to the live clause extractor. Assert that the returned structured output contains entries for each category with correct text spans. Run multiple contracts to account for non-determinism; fail if any required term category is absent or the identified text is substantively wrong.

behaviors.description: The risk assessor flags language that would be problematic under the configured risk policy, even when the problematic phrasing is indirect or euphemistic.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This is a single reviewer's detection quality. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed. LLM-DEP: The qualifier 'indirect or euphemistic' is the critical phrase — it explicitly tests the AI's semantic reasoning under ambiguity. A stub always passes because you predefine its response; it cannot reveal that the model fails to recognize that 'subject to adjustment at sole discretion' is a risk flag. The only way to expose this failure is with a real model call. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: Wiring modules together does nothing to reveal whether the LLM recognizes euphemistic risk language. The failure boundary is squarely in the model's classification quality.
behaviors.test_description: Present the live risk assessor with a contract containing both directly stated risk language ('unlimited liability') and euphemistic equivalents ('obligations may be revisited at the party's convenience'). Under the configured risk policy, assert both are flagged. Test across several indirect phrasings drawn from real contract drafting conventions. Fail if any known-risky euphemism is not flagged.

behaviors.description: The compliance checker correctly determines whether the contract meets all applicable regulatory requirements for the configured jurisdiction.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Single reviewer output quality. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network dependency. LLM-DEP: Correctly applying jurisdiction-specific regulatory requirements requires the model to have and apply knowledge of those requirements. Whether the model correctly identifies a GDPR data-processing clause as compliant or non-compliant depends on real LLM output — a stub answers whatever you script. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: Module wiring cannot reveal whether the model knows the regulatory requirements for a jurisdiction. The failure mode lives entirely in AI reasoning quality.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the compliance checker for a specific jurisdiction (e.g., GDPR/EU). Supply the live checker with contracts that are (a) fully compliant, (b) missing a required clause, and (c) containing a clause that violates a specific rule. Assert the checker returns correct pass/fail verdicts and identifies the specific failing clause in cases (b) and (c). Repeat for at least one additional jurisdiction to verify jurisdiction-switching works.

behaviors.description: When two reviewers flag the same clause with conflicting assessments (e.g., extractor says 'standard' but risk assessor says 'high risk'), the merge step preserves both assessments with attribution.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is in the merge function's conflict-resolution logic, not in any module handoff. Given two structured reviewer outputs that conflict on the same clause ID, does the merge function produce an output record that contains both assessments with correct attribution labels? That is entirely a local algorithmic question. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network/subprocess. LLM-DEP: No — the merge step receives already-structured reviewer outputs; its correctness is deterministic given fixed inputs. A stub that returns conflicting structured records is a faithful stand-in because the failure we are testing is in the merge code, not in any AI output. MOCK-HIDE: Providing deterministic conflicting inputs does not hide the merge failure; it directly exercises it. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: There is no module boundary whose interaction introduces this failure. The merge function either preserves both assessments or it does not, testable with in-process constructed inputs.
behaviors.test_description: Construct two structured reviewer-output objects (no real LLM calls) that each reference the same clause ID but carry conflicting assessment labels ('standard' vs 'high risk'). Call the merge function directly. Assert the returned unified record contains exactly two assessment entries for that clause, each bearing the correct reviewer attribution, with no data dropped or overwritten.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — dispatching to all three reviewers, collecting results, merging into a unified report — completes correctly for a real contract without losing any individual reviewer's findings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: 'Without losing any individual reviewer's findings' is an end-to-end data-integrity claim that only surfaces when all three real AI steps run and their outputs flow through the collection and merge stages. REAL-INFRA: No unique OS/subprocess dependency beyond the AI calls. LLM-DEP: Each of the three reviewers is an AI model call; results vary across runs and must be faithfully preserved through collection and merge. MULTI-STEP: Yes — three parallel agentic steps (clause extractor, risk assessor, compliance checker) followed by a sequential merge. This is the canonical Workflow pattern: multiple real agentic steps in sequence/parallel feeding a downstream stage. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: A single-reviewer Agentic test cannot reveal failures in parallel dispatch, result collection, or data loss at the merge boundary. The failure mode requires all three real AI calls to complete and their outputs to survive the pipeline. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: Stubbing the three reviewers would test orchestration plumbing but cannot catch a real case where one reviewer's findings are silently dropped because of an edge case in how the merge handles the actual structured output that reviewer produces.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a representative real contract to the full pipeline with all three live AI reviewers. After completion, assert: (1) the unified report contains sections attributed to all three reviewers, (2) no clause identified by any individual reviewer is absent from the unified report, and (3) the total finding count in the report is at least the union of findings from each reviewer run independently. Run twice to check for non-deterministic data loss.
